reasoning.typography: Geist typeface system is executed well—disciplined, technical character fits the dev tool brand. Size scale is confident, but still within 'developer tool' conventions rather than truly distinctive.
reasoning.hierarchy: Hero headline dominates effectively, gradient pyramid draws focus. However, below-fold content becomes more democratic/uniform—the testimonial section especially lacks visual dominance between elements.
reasoning.distinctiveness: The gradient pyramid and 'AI Cloud' positioning are Vercel-specific, but structural beats (testimonials, product grid, feature cards) are replaceable. Swap 'Vercel' for 'Netlify' and much still works—it's product-category design.
reasoning.color: Black base with blue-to-red gradient is restrained and striking. Accent colors (teal event badge, gradient hero) stay under 10% of canvas. No decorative noise—disciplined execution.
reasoning.density: Hero has confident breathing room. Mid-page toggles between dense (testimonials tight) and loose (feature cards padded). Not generic uniform padding, but also not systematically intentional—varies by section.
reasoning.voice: Structure is 'Hero / Social Proof / Product Grid / Features'—textbook SaaS stencil. The copy has personality ('build times went from 7m to 40s') but the IA doesn't reflect Vercel's actual narrative—could be any dev platform.

winning_patterns: Gradient pyramid visual is memorable and technically expressive—fits AI infrastructure positioning
Geist typeface system executed with discipline—appropriate character for developer tools
Color restraint: black canvas with surgical gradient accent creates technical premium feel
Testimonial specificity: '7m to 40s' and '24x faster' are concrete, not generic fluff
Hero type scale is confident—large enough to do visual work without secondary elements
anti_patterns: Below-fold becomes product-grid stencil: 'Your product / Agents / AI Apps / Web Apps' tiles are interchangeable
IA is textbook SaaS template—doesn't surface what makes Vercel's story unique vs. competitors
Feature cards have uniform treatment—no visual prioritization of what matters most
Event banner ('Ship 26 coming to 5 cities') sits in nav limbo—not integrated into story hierarchy
Tab navigation ('AI Apps / Web Apps / Ecommerce') feels like feature-list thinking, not narrative structure
